cataloging_subject.label: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The 1827 edited compilation attributed in library catalogs to Nicholas Harris Nicolas and Edward Tyrrell: an anonymous civic chronicle whose actual narrative spans 1189-1483, editorial notes and manuscript variants, and appended contemporary letters and poems. CME00006 represents only the title page, printed pp. 1-147, and selected illustrative matter on pp. 216-253. This record keeps the compilation distinct from its component medieval works and documents, the 1827 printed edition, the U-M electronic selection, the repository XML, and every manuscript witness.

catalog_summary.title: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous London chronicle compilers; John Lydgate and John Bocking for identified or attributed components
catalog_summary.editor: Nicholas Harris Nicolas and Edward Tyrrell
catalog_summary.date: London Chronicle compiled in stages in the early fifteenth century and continued to 1483; edited compilation first published 1827
catalog_summary.region: England, especially London; exact place of compilation is not established for every component

titles.value: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483
notes: The title's 1089 is a historical error retained as part of the established title; the chronicle begins with Richard I's coronation in 1189.

titles.value: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483; Written in the Fifteenth Century, and for the First Time Printed from MSS. in the British Museum: To Which Are Added Numerous Contemporary Illustrations, Consisting of Royal Letters, Poems, and Other Articles Descriptive of Public Events, or of the Manners and Customs of the Metropolis

notes: A discovery title that corrects the narrative's opening year without silently altering the 1827 publication title.

agents.name: Anonymous compilers and continuators of the London Chronicle
notes: Kingsford distinguishes multiple evolving London-chronicle versions; neither the Harley copy nor the later Cotton continuation supports assignment to one named author.

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: The British Library dates him c. 1370-1449/50. Attribution differs by component and is modeled separately below.

scope.description: The civic chronicle and its successive continuations through 1483

notes: The 1827 preface infers one original compiler and one continuator, while Kingsford's manuscript comparison demonstrates a more complex sequence of versions.
Tyrrell signs the dedication; modern library records credit him jointly with Nicolas.

scope.description: Selection, arrangement, transcription, and annotation of the appended illustrative materials
Selection, arrangement, transcription, and annotation of the appended illustrative materials

assertion.notes: The 1827 edition names Lydgate, but the current British Library component description calls this simply an English poem and does not repeat the attribution.

notes: The Harley colophon attributes the making to the monk of Bury.

scope.description: The reconciliation poem, not Bocking's letter

assertion.notes: The 1827 editorial introduction invokes Lydgate, but the current British Library component record identifies the poem and folios without naming an author.

scope.description: Letter to Sir John Fastolf dated 15 March 1457/8

date_statements.display: The English London Chronicle tradition was first compiled in the early fifteenth century; the Harley version was brought through 1443
notes: This describes textual compilation, not the age of every event narrated or every later manuscript copy.

date_statements.display: Harley MS 565, written c. 1444 and carrying the chronicle through 1443

assertion.notes: This is a witness-production date and terminus for its version, not a single composition date for the whole chronicle.

date_statements.display: Continuation through Edward IV's death in 1483, preserved in Cotton MS Julius B I copied soon afterward

scope.description: The later continuation beyond the Harley ending

assertion.notes: The chronicle continues through events of 1483. Kingsford says this copy was made probably soon after Edward IV's death, while the British Library catalogs the relevant chronicle in the last quarter of the fifteenth century; neither statement supports a tighter normalized production range.

date_statements.display: 15 March 1457/8 (15 March 1458 in modern New Style dating)
notes: The 1827 heading's 1457 follows historical English year reckoning; Fenn explains the Mid-Lent calculation, and the current BL catalog normalizes the date to 1458.

date_statements.display: First published as the Nicolas-Tyrrell compilation in 1827

notes: This broad cultural origin does not force every selected document or manuscript witness into one exact place of composition.
The chronicle and editorial selection center London but also narrate events elsewhere in England, the British Isles, and France.
Civic organization by mayoral year and London subject matter support a strong association; this is not a claim that Harley MS 565 was physically copied in London.

scope.description: Reign headings, marginal matter, manuscript variants, and documentary illustrations

language_statements.label: Old French

scope.description: The 1296 French poem and related medieval documentary matter

notes: The 1827 preface calls the item French; this record uses the broad ISO 639-3 historical-language label rather than assigning a narrower dialect without further linguistic analysis.

scope.description: Title, dedication, preface, translations, editorial introductions, and notes

form_statements.description: A prose civic chronicle and prose documentary/editorial matter accompanied by substantial Middle English and multilingual verse selections.
Predominantly prose annalistic narrative, with Latin headings and nine short verse lines represented in the XML.
Substantial civic, historical, and satirical poems selected as illustrations.

genre_statements.label: Scholarly edition
notes: An edition-level genre; it does not describe the medieval chronicle or letters as nineteenth-century scholarship.

summaries.value: A composite antiquarian edition centered on an anonymous London civic chronicle arranged by reign and mayoral year. Its chronicle begins in 1189 despite the title's 1089, follows London officeholders and events through 1483, and is supplemented by manuscript variants, civic records, royal and private letters, and historical poems, many associated with John Lydgate.

The 274-page edition prints the main chronicle on pp. 1-147, Cotton manuscript variants and additional Harley texts on pp. 149-190, documentary and poetic illustrations on pp. 191-274, and a Harley fol. 37 facsimile plate in the preliminaries. CME00006 transcribes the title page, pp. 1-147, and only pp. 216-253 of the illustrations.

The 1827 volume's anonymous chronicle, Lydgate poems, Bocking letter, archival documents, editorial notes, printed facsimile, U-M selection, and manuscript witnesses are separate intellectual or material layers. Dates, authorship, folios, access, and image terms are asserted only at the layer supported by each source.

extent.description: The six sections are cataloging-level macro-parts of the 274-page printed compilation, not authorial divisions. The verse counts are XML representation metrics: 154 LG and 1,205 L elements in CME00006, including 67/592 in the Henry V expedition poem, 77/540 in the Henry VI entry poem, 8/64 in the reconciliation poem, and 2/9 embedded in the chronicle. The XML has 185 PB elements and omits substantial printed ranges.

content_parts.label: Anonymous London Chronicle, 1189-1483
content_parts.summary: A predominantly prose civic chronicle arranged by reign and mayoral year. The 1827 text follows Harley MS 565 through its broken ending in 1443, records Cotton Julius B I variants, and prints the Cotton continuation through 1483.
notes: DIV3 count is an encoding metric for annual containers and must not be treated as a canonical count of historical years or documents.

content_parts.label: Manuscript variants, notes, and early documentary illustrations
content_parts.summary: Cotton Julius B I variants, Harley transcripts and translations of St Paul's tablets, and Latin, French, and English civic and royal documents preceding the long poems.
notes: This entire printed range is omitted from CME00006.

content_parts.label: Poem on Henry V's French expedition, Agincourt, and return to London
content_parts.summary: A three-passus Middle English historical poem preserved in Harley MS 565 and attributed to Lydgate by the 1827 editors.

content_parts.label: The Coming of King Henry VI from France to London
content_parts.summary: John Lydgate's Middle English account of Henry VI's triumphal entry into London after his French coronation, preserved in Harley MS 565.

content_parts.label: Materials on the 1458 reconciliation of Henry VI and the Yorkist lords
content_parts.summary: An editorial note, an excerpt from John Bocking's letter to Sir John Fastolf, and two manuscript versions of the reconciliation poem. CME00006 includes the note, letter, and Cotton Nero A VI version but omits the Cotton Vespasian B XVI version.
notes: The XML verse metrics count only the encoded Cotton Nero version.

content_parts.label: Later Lydgate and Lydgate-attributed ballads
content_parts.summary: The concluding ballads, including material on civic dining, London Lickpenny, the empty purse, horned headdresses, and fraudulent millers and bakers.
notes: This concluding range is omitted from CME00006 and individual attributions should be checked against current poem-level scholarship before being expanded.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; EEBO/IDG; title page; chronicle; appended DIV1 elements
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00006 and preserves the 1827 title page, main chronicle, two long Harley poems, a Bocking letter extract, one reconciliation-poem version, page milestones, notes, and the 2016 structural-review statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00006.
evidence.locator: Item information, rights, contents, and full text
evidence.summary: Confirms the title, London 1827 imprint, public text, stable identifier, London-history and social-customs subjects, and public-domain / No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: Nicholas Harris Nicolas and Edward Tyrrell, eds., A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483 (London: Longman, Rees, Orme, Brown, and Green, 1827), Internet Archive item chronicleoflondo00nicouoft.
evidence.locator: Facsimile plate; title page; dedication; preface pp. v-viii; chronicle pp. 1-147; notes pp. 149-190; illustrations pp. 191-274
evidence.summary: Complete primary edition evidence for title, editorial responsibility, two principal chronicle manuscripts, source selection, collation, title-date error, printed boundaries, component attributions, documentary sources, and material omitted by CME00006.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 565.
evidence.summary: Current exact catalog for the complete parchment codex, c. 1444: the main Chronicle of London on ff. 10r-99r, St Paul's tablet transcripts, the Henry V expedition poem, Lydgate's Henry VI entry poem, codex extent, origin, and currently unavailable legacy digital images.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Julius B I and component ff. 2-98.
evidence.locator: Chronicle ff. 2r-90v; complete codex physical description and access
evidence.summary: Current exact component and parent records for the paper Chronicle of the City of London, 1189-1483, its later contents, London origin, last-quarter-fifteenth-century date, complete physical extent, and absence of digitized content.

evidence.citation: Charles Lethbridge Kingsford, ed., Chronicles of London (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1905), introduction pp. vi-xxiii, especially pp. viii-xv.
evidence.locator: Introduction pp. vi-xxiii; Harley 565 pp. x-xi; Cotton Julius B I pp. xiii-xv
evidence.summary: Comparative manuscript study establishing the evolving London-chronicle tradition, early-fifteenth-century compilation, Harley c. 1443/4 version, Cotton continuation and copying date, and the exact way Nicolas and Tyrrell used both witnesses.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Nero A VI, ff. 71-197.
evidence.locator: Reconciliation of Henry VI with the Yorkists, ff. 196v-197v
evidence.summary: Identifies the exact Middle English reconciliation-poem component, its folios, broader codex contents and date, and physical/request access without public digitized images.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 43488, Paston Letters and Papers.
evidence.locator: f. 41, John Bocking to Sir John Fastolf, 15 March 1458
evidence.summary: Current catalog identifies the original letter, modern date, sender, recipient, exact folio, 59-folio bound volume, paper support, and currently unavailable legacy digitization.

evidence.citation: John Fenn, ed., Original Letters, Written during the Reigns of Henry VI, Edward IV, and Richard III, vol. 1 (London: G. G. J. and J. Robinson, 1787).
evidence.summary: Immediate printed source cited by the 1827 edition for Bocking's letter; prints facsimile-style and normalized texts, explains that Mid-Lent places it in historical 1457/8, and supplies the wording excerpted on p. 251 of the Chronicle.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of General Corpus CME00006.xml using lxml element paths and printed-page milestones.
evidence.locator: EEBO/TEXT/FRONT and BODY; DIV1-DIV3, PB, P, LG, L, and NOTE
evidence.summary: Counts 7 DIV1, 15 DIV2, 295 DIV3, 185 PB, 312 P, 154 LG, 1,205 L, and 632 NOTE elements; establishes encoded printed-page spans 1-147 and 216-253, with no PB numbered 234, and component verse metrics.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00006.
evidence.summary: Binds descriptive claims to the repository XML, U-M encoding, 1827 edition, manuscript witnesses, later comparison edition, facsimiles, practical access, and rights evidence.

open_questions.question: What is the current scholarly attribution status of the Henry V expedition poem and the 1458 reconciliation poem beyond the 1827 editors' assignment to Lydgate?
next_steps: Consult the current DIMEV records keyed to Boffey numbers 969 and 3929 and recent Lydgate canon scholarship.
Retain component-specific attributed status rather than assigning Lydgate as author of the whole compilation.

open_questions.question: Which exact folios of Cotton MS Julius B I carry the 1443-1483 continuation printed on pp. 133-147?
next_steps: Consult a folio-keyed modern description or the manuscript when the complete images return.
Do not narrow the current ff. 2r-90v chronicle extent by inference from page count.

open_questions.question: What production history and selection policy caused CME00006 to omit the 1827 preliminaries, pp. 149-215, and pp. 254-274?
next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source delivery package from U-M.
Continue to describe CME00006 as a selected encoding, not a complete transcription of the 1827 volume.

notes: A complete 1827 edition scan and several supporting sources are cached locally; manuscript-image completeness and redistribution terms are handled only in the lineage manifest.
The exact preferred title is retained for bibliographic identity even though its opening year is demonstrably wrong.
